description: Interactive multi-mode tool for NCERT Class 6 Science (Curiosity, Methods of Separation of Substances). Students learn the winnowing rule, predict whether wind will separate five real mixtures (with immediate weight-difference feedback and scoring), explore real-world uses, and toss mixtures into the wind in a hands-on canvas physics simulator. Core idea: wind separates a mixture only when its two parts differ enough in WEIGHT.
subject: Science
chapter: Chapter 9 — Methods of Separation of Substances
activity: Winnowing — separating a mixture by weight using moving air
concepts: Why some mixtures need sieving instead of wind

themeColor.description: Accent colour seed (hex). Per-mode tab gradients are defined internally.

initialMode.description: Which mode the tool opens on. Falls back to the first enabled mode if this mode is not enabled.

showModeSelector.description: Show or hide the top mode-tab bar. Hide it to lock the tool to a single mode.

enabledModes.description: Ordered list of modes to expose as tabs. Order also drives the step counter (currentStep / totalSteps).

showScore.description: Show the live score chip on the Predict board.

shuffle.description: Randomise the order of the prediction mixtures on load.

additionalProps.description: Tool-specific dynamic content passed by the agent to customise the simulation. Values here override the top-level parameters of the same name.

mixtures.description: Override the prediction set. Each item is a MixtureDef.

itemShape.name: string — display name, e.g. 'Wheat grains + husk'

showScore.description: Same as the top-level showScore; provided here for convenience.

enabledModes.description: Override which mode tabs are shown.

mixtures.name: Wheat grains + husk
mixtures.reason: Husk is thin and light — wind lifts it easily and the heavy grain falls straight down.

learn.title: Learn the rule
learn.description: Three concept cards introduce winnowing, the weight-gap rule, and when winnowing fails (two heavy parts → use a sieve).

predict.title: Predict the winnowing result
predict.description: For each of five mixtures the student chooses Separates well / partly / does not separate. On answer the tray animates to show the actual separation and a weight-based explanation appears. Live score and a summary banner after all five.

real_world.title: Real-world uses
real_world.description: Four cards: grain from chaff on farms, cleaning puffed rice/poha, grading tea and spices by weight, and why sand + pebbles need a sieve instead.
cards: Sand and pebbles (why size fails here)

hands_on.title: Winnow it yourself
hands_on.description: Canvas physics sandbox. Pick a mixture and press Toss; gravity drops every bit while a steady breeze pushes each toward a terminal horizontal speed set by its mass. Light bits blow aside, heavy bits drop nearly straight down, and near-equal weights fall together.
features: requestAnimationFrame physics loop (gravity + mass-based wind drag)
Fixed gentle breeze (4 / 10) with animated wind arrows
Zone labels: 'heavy falls here' vs 'light blows here'
Mixture selector reuses the five default pairs

animations: tray particle transitions — bits ease into separated piles on reveal
designSystem.notes: All particle art (peanut, husk, dal, rice, etc.) is hand-built inline SVG, and icons are inline SVG — no external icon library, so the tool renders standalone.

teachConcept.description: Full guided flow: learn → predict → real world → winnow it

assessOnly.description: Locked prediction quiz with shuffled mixtures for revision

handsOnSim.description: Jump straight into the wind-toss simulator

introCards.description: Concept + real-world cards only, no interaction

ncertAlignment.keyConceptTaught: Winnowing uses moving air to separate a mixture, but only when the two parts differ enough in weight. A big weight gap separates cleanly, a small gap separates partly, and two heavy parts do not separate by wind — they need sieving by size.
learningObjectives: Define winnowing as separation using wind / moving air
Explain that winnowing depends on a difference in weight between components
Predict whether a given mixture will separate well, partly, or not at all
Recognise when sieving (by size) is needed instead of winnowing
Relate winnowing to real-life uses (grain and chaff, puffed rice, tea grading)
Practise the inquiry cycle: predict, observe the result, conclude
